title: Ongoing Supplier Monitoring and Performance
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A senior QP guide to ongoing supplier monitoring: risk-based metrics, QMS integration, escalation and CAPA to keep your pharma supply chain inspection-ready.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette against deep navy, with a single subtle green accent light. A close, shallow depth-of-field view of a gloved hand reviewing a translucent quality dashboard or row of glass sample vials on a polished laboratory bench, soft natural light raking from one side, fine condensation and clean reflective surfaces, premium and precise mood, sense of careful inspection and oversight, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: How often should suppliers be monitored versus formally re-qualified?
faqs.a: Performance monitoring should be continuous, drawing on goods-in, QC, complaint and deviation data as it arises, with a periodic scorecard review at a frequency set by risk. Formal re-qualification or audit cycles are typically risk-based, with higher-risk suppliers reviewed more frequently. The monitoring data itself should inform whether a re-qualification or for-cause audit is brought forward.

faqs.q: Which suppliers need the most intensive monitoring?
faqs.a: Apply your risk classification: suppliers of sterile products, active substances and anything subject to Annex 1 or direct MHRA oversight generally warrant the highest intensity. Sole-source suppliers and those with a history of deviations or complaints also justify closer scrutiny. Lower-risk suppliers, such as some secondary packaging vendors, can be monitored more lightly, provided the rationale is documented.

faqs.q: What should a supplier scorecard actually measure?
faqs.a: Focus on a small, risk-relevant set rather than measuring everything. Core indicators usually cover quality (right-first-time, rejection and out-of-specification rates), delivery (on-time-in-full and lead-time variability), and compliance (CAPA timeliness, complaint trends and licence currency). Each metric needs a defined threshold and an owner so that breaches trigger a clear escalation route.
